narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile—including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, LSAT score of 22, first-year school decile of 1st, third-year school decile of 1st, and your plan to attend full-time—and calculated an 85.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction, in order of strongest influence, were your third-year grades, your LSAT score, and your first-year grades—all of which signaled lower academic performance relative to what the model associates with bar exam success. Your third-year decile ranking in the bottom 10% of your school (far below the typical 5th or 6th decile) had the single strongest negative pull, strongly contributing to the failure prediction by highlighting a concerning drop or stagnation in performance. Next, your LSAT score of 22—which is well below the average of around 37—further reinforced the model's concern, as higher scores are key indicators of bar readiness in past students. Your first-year decile of 1st (again, much lower than the average around 6th) added to this, though with somewhat less weight, painting a picture of consistently below-average early academic standing compared to peers who typically pass. While your full-time enrollment plan aligns with most students (over 90%) and your middle-income background is common, these didn't offset the dominant impact of your grades and test scores. Overall, your profile stands out as weaker academically than the typical admitted student, whose stronger high school deciles and LSATs correlate with bar passage rates above 80%. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven flag based on patterns from similar students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or additional coursework, and perhaps exploring part-time options or prep programs proven to help students in the bottom deciles succeed on the bar. With focused improvements in these areas, you could reapply stronger next cycle and shift the model's prediction in your favor.
